coredata.dc:title: Preferential photochemical interaction of Ru (III) doped carbon nano dots with bovine serum albumin over human serum albumin
coredata.dc:creator: Maity, Arnab
Pal, Uttam
Chakraborty, Brotati
Sengupta, Chaitrali
Sau, Abhishek
Chakraborty, Swatadipta
Basu, Samita
coredata.dc:description: The excitation wavelength dependent emission of carbon nano dots (CNDs) restricts their use in photophysical studies. However, instead of bare CNDs, the amine coated Ru (III) doped CNDs (Ru:CNDEDAs) are quite eligible to generate excitation wavelength independent fluorescence with high quantum yield. Herein, we report a detailed study on the photochemical interaction between two different serum albumins, bovine serum albumin (BSA) and human serum albumin (HSA), with Ru:CNDEDAs synthesized in our laboratory, using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopic techniques. Absorption study reveals the formation of ground state complex between Ru:CNDEDAs and BSA/HSA while the circular dichroism study implies that Ru:CNDEDAs perturbs the secondary structure of the albumin proteins. Steady-state fluorescence study helps in understanding energy transfer from tryptophan, the fluorophore moiety of BSA and HSA, to Ru:CNDEDAs. Time-resolved studies within nanosecond time domain clarify the phenomenon of energy transfer from BSA/HSA to Ru:CNDEDAs with varied efficiency. Molecular dynamic simulation ascertains that the efficiency of energy transfer is highly dependent on the stability of protein-nanoparticle complex. This study provides a qualitative description regarding the structural rigidity of transport protein, BSA compared to HSA, which determines the transport ability of CNDs to deliver the desired drug molecule to the targeted cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nano dots
Serum albumin
Fluorescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the Biomolecular Assembly, Recognition and Dynamics (BARD) project, SINP of Department of Atomic Energy (DAE), Government of India is greatly acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular models of BSA and HSA bound to the surface of amine coated carbon nano particle. Inset shows the size comparison protein with carbon nano particle. Two tryptophans (trp) in BSA and single trp in HSA are marked. Proteins are shown in ribbon representation and C → N terminal is colored in rainbow. Nano surface is shown in CPK space-filling model with standard color code for atoms. C in bottom layer is colored green. Tryptophan residues are also shown in CPK.

(a) Absorption spectra of BSA protein and (b) HSA protein with increasing concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs in PBS buffer. Inset shows the relative change in absorbance at 360 nm with gradually increasing concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs from 0 to 2.5 μM.

Circular dichroism spectral changes of BSA (a) and HSA (b) on interaction with increasing concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs from 0 to 8.34 μM. Insets of (a) and (b) are the magnified region of 200–230 nm of the respective proteins.

Changes in the emission intensity of (a) BSA and (b) HSA with gradual increase in concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs from 0 to 2.5 μM in PBS buffer and excitation at 280 nm. In this experiment 2 μM concentration of BSA and HSA proteins has been used.
(a) and (b) Show SV plots of BSA and HSA protein respectively with increasing concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs and insets show same SV plots in lower concentration range of Ru:CNDEDAs.
Overall structural changes in BSA (red) and HSA (green) on the nano surface from that of the initial configuration over the course of time (48 ns).
Changes in the radius of gyration of BSA (blue) and HSA (red) on the carbon nano surface over the course of time (48 ns).

Fluctuations of the backbone and side chain residues of (a) BSA and (b) HSA. (a) Amino acid backbone and side chain fluctuations in BSA when bound to the nano surface (yellow) in comparison to that of the free BSA in solution (blue). Positions of the two tryptophan residues are marked with vertical dotted lines. (b) Amino acid backbone and side chain fluctuations in HSA when bound to the nano surface in comparison to that of the free HSA in solution. Position of the tryptophan residue is marked with a vertical dotted line.

Changes in the secondary structure of BSA (a) and HSA (b) over time. Top panel of (a) shows the changes in the total helix (blue) and coil (yellow) content in BSA when bound to the nano surface and bottom panel shows the breaking and making of helix (red) in different regions of BSA over 48 ns of simulation. Top panel of (b) shows the changes in the total helix and coil content in HSA when bound to the nano surface and bottom panel shows the breaking and making of helix in different regions of HSA over 48 ns of simulation.

Comparison of the secondary structural frequencies of different regions of BSA (a) and HSA (b) between the nano bound form and free form. (a) Changes in the helix and coil propensities of different regions of BSA when bound to the nano surface (yellow) than that of the free BSA (blue). Positions of the two tryptophan residues are marked with vertical dotted lines. (b) Changes in the helix and coil propensities of different regions of HSA when bound to the nano surface (yellow) than that of the free HSA (blue). Position of the tryptophan residue is marked with a vertical dotted line.
Distances from tryptophan residues to the nano surface. Yellow, Trp158 of BSA; blue, Trp237 of BSA; red, Trp238 of HSA.
Variation of α-helix% of BSA in phosphate buffer with increasing concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs.
Variation of α‑helix% of HSA in phosphate buffer with increasing concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs.

Fluorescence lifetime of BSA protein in absence and presence of Ru:CNDEDAs. (λ
ex = 295 nm and λ
em = 350 nm).

Fluorescence lifetime of HSA protein in absence and presence of Ru:CNDEDAs. (λ
ex = 295 nm and λ
em = 350 nm).

Fluorescence lifetime of BSA (5 μM) protein in absence and presence of Ru:CNDEDAs. (λ
ex = 295 nm and λ
em = 450 nm).

Fluorescence lifetime of HSA (5 μM) protein in absence and presence of Ru:CNDEDAs. (λ
ex = 295 nm and λ
em = 450 nm).

Calculation of K
SV and k
q from steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence data for both Ru:CNDEDA-BSA and Ru:CNDEDA-HSA systems.
Values of binding and thermodynamic parameters for Ru:CNDEDA-BSA system at different temperatures.
Values of binding and thermodynamic parameters for Ru:CNDEDA-HSA system at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Preferential photochemical interaction of Ru (III) doped carbon nano dots with bovine serum albumin over human serum albumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Akal University, Talwandi Sabo, Bhatinda, Punjab 151302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Akal University, Talwandi Sabo, Bhatinda, Punjab-151302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: S.N. Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences, Kolkata, West Bengal PIN-700106, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: S.N. Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: S.N. Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences, Kolkata, West Bengal, PIN- 700106, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Bejoy Narayan Mahavidyalaya, Itachuna, Hooghly, West Bengal, PIN 712147, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Sciences Division, Saha Institute of Nuclear Physics, 1/AF Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700064, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Sciences Division, Saha Institute of Nuclear Physics, 1/AF Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700064, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The excitation wavelength dependent emission of carbon nano dots (CNDs) restricts their use in photophysical studies. However, instead of bare CNDs, the amine coated Ru (III) doped CNDs (Ru:CNDEDAs) are quite eligible to generate excitation wavelength independent fluorescence with high quantum yield. Herein, we report a detailed study on the photochemical interaction between two different serum albumins, bovine serum albumin (BSA) and human serum albumin (HSA), with Ru:CNDEDAs synthesized in our laboratory, using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopic techniques. Absorption study reveals the formation of ground state complex between Ru:CNDEDAs and BSA/HSA while the circular dichroism study implies that Ru:CNDEDAs perturbs the secondary structure of the albumin proteins. Steady-state fluorescence study helps in understanding energy transfer from tryptophan, the fluorophore moiety of BSA and HSA, to Ru:CNDEDAs. Time-resolved studies within nanosecond time domain clarify the phenomenon of energy transfer from BSA/HSA to Ru:CNDEDAs with varied efficiency. Molecular dynamic simulation ascertains that the efficiency of energy transfer is highly dependent on the stability of protein-nanoparticle complex. This study provides a qualitative description regarding the structural rigidity of transport protein, BSA compared to HSA, which determines the transport ability of CNDs to deliver the desired drug molecule to the targeted cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Since last few decades nanoparticles have gained significant interest owing to their unique properties useful for biomedical and chemical applications. In fact, in recent years, nanomaterials are being used as potential competitors to traditional fluorescent dye molecules and subsequently this field of research is developing fast due to extensive demand of fluorescent probe in chemical sensing, biological monitoring, electronic devices and other related fields [1]. The quantum size and other unique effects provide higher stability, slower photobleaching, stronger fluorescence intensity, etc. to the fluorescent nanomaterials compared to the traditional fluorescent dye molecules [2]. Among different kind of nanoparticles the carbon based quantum dots have become the focus of attention because of their chemical stability, excellent water solubility, tuneable fluorescent properties, low cost, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and environment-friendly nature [3–6]. Owing to considerable surface activity, nanoparticles interact immediately with cell surface of proteins, DNA, serum proteins, carbohydrates, etc. while introduced to living systems [7–9]. Recent study of protein interaction with flat surface indicates that distortion of protein takes place upon adsorption that provides a new class of biological entity called ‘nanoparticle protein corona’ (NP-PC) in the biological milieu [10–18]. The overall NP-PC formation is a multifunctional process and depends not only on the characteristics of the nano particle but also on the interacting proteins and media [19]. Irreversible binding of protein on surface of nanoparticles leads to the formation of ‘hard corona’ whereas quick or reversible binding of proteins on nanoparticles with faster exchange rates produces ‘soft corona’ [20–24]. Of late much attention has been paid to the interaction of nanoparticles with plasma proteins [25]. New biomimetic nanoparticles coated with membrane directly derived from RBCs have been synthesized by Zhang et al. [26] and that provides a major breakthrough in nano particle drug delivery system. Such membrane coated nanoparticles have prolonged ‘particle systemic circulation half life’ than corresponding PEGylated system. It has been reported that gold nanoparticles with increasing concentration are capable of influencing the conformational change of bovine serum albumin (BSA) [27]. Jungbauer et al. thoroughly investigated the effect of size of nanoparticles on the conformational change of BSA and myoglobin proteins adsorbed on their surface [28]. Liu et al. prepared amino functionalized carbon dots (CDs) which show green fluorescence at various excitation wavelength and are also able to transfer surface energy to negatively charged hyaluronate stabilized gold nanoparticles [29].

Carbon nano dots (CNDs) are presently being used in biotechnology, imaging, sensing, preparation of light emitting devices, etc. [29,30,31]. Excitation wavelength dependent emission property of CNDs hinders them from being used in many photo physical studies. Our group has recently reported that the excitation-dependent photoluminescence of CNDs can be tuned to yield excitation-independent highly fluorescent nanodots by doping with Ru (III) and coating with ethylenediamine [32,33]. Ru (III) doped amine coated CNDs (Ru:CNDEDAs) thus fulfil the requirement of a normal fluorescent dye molecule with reasonable quantum yield. Study of literature suggests that the photoluminescence of Ru:CNDEDAs is quenched by photoinduced electron transfer or energy transfer with quinone drug, menadione [34–36]. These findings indicate that the studies on interactions between CNDs and blood proteins might eventually help to clarify the impact of CNDs on health as well as related cellular trafficking and systematic translocation, which in turn, can provide a promising therapeutic approach.

Presently, BSA and human serum albumin (HSA) are considered as most widely used model proteins. Here, we like to present a detailed study on the photochemical interaction between the two serum albumins and Ru:CNDEDAs synthesized in our laboratory using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopic techniques, viz., UV–Vis absorption, steady-state fluorescence, circular dichroism (CD), time-correlated-single-photon counting (TCSPC) for fluorescence lifetime measurements within nanosecond time domain. The interaction pattern of carbon nanoparticles with BSA/HSA protein is further studied using molecular dynamics (MD) simulation to corroborate our experimental findings.

All the chemicals and solvents required for syntheses of Ru:CDs and Ru:CNDEDAs are of analytical grade. Citric acid (CA) (≥99.5%), Ruthenium (III) chloride hydrate and Ethylene diamine (EDA) (≥99%) have been purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Tris buffer, sodium chloride and hydrochloric acid have been procured from Merck and used without further purification. Triple distilled water is used for the preparation of all the aqueous solutions. Solvents required for syntheses and spectroscopic studies have been purchased from SRL. Fatty acid and globulin-free BSA and HSA have been obtained from Sigma Chemical Co.

Ru:CDs are synthesized by pyrolyzing the mixture of citric acid (1 g) and RuCl3 (0.005 g) at 250 °C in water as reported earlier by Bera et al. [32,33]. A stepwise treatment of Ru(0.005):CDs with ethylene diamine (EDA) at 120 °C in DMF results in N-doped (amine coated) carbon nano dots, Ru:CNDEDAs [32,33].

The concentrations of the solutions of BSA and HSA in tris buffer are determined spectrophotometrically using ε280 = 44,300 M−1 cm−1 for BSA and ε280 = 35,353 M−1 cm−1 for HSA [37], where ε280 is the molar extinction coefficient of the respective proteins at 280 nm.

Steady-state absorption and fluorescence spectra of Ru:CNDEDA - BSA/HSA interactions are recorded by using JASCO V-650 absorption spectrophotometer and Hitachi F-7000 Spectro-fluorimeter respectively using a 1.0 cm path length quartz cuvette. Stern-Volmer (SV) equation [38] is used for the analysis of fluorescence data.
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
Q
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
_x0001_where F0 and F denote fluorescence intensities of protein in the absence and presence of Ru:CNDEDAs, respectively, K
sv is the SV quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs. Here k
q represents the bi-molecular quenching constant and τ
0 is the lifetime of the fluorophore in absence of quencher.

Time-resolved emission spectra of BSA/HSA in absence and presence of Ru:CNDEDAs are recorded using a picosecond pulsed diode laser based TCSPC fluorescence spectrometer with λ
ex = 295 nm and MCP-PMT as a detector [39,40]. The emission from the samples is collected at a right angle to the direction of the excitation beam maintaining magic angle polarization (54.7°) with a bandpass of 2 nm. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the instrument response function is 970 ps and the resolution is 28 ps per channel. The data are fitted to exponential functions after deconvolution of the instrument response function by an iterative reconvolution technique using data analysis software in which reduced χ 2 and weighted residuals serve as parameters for goodness of fit. All the steady-state and time-resolved measurements are performed at room temperature (298 K) unless otherwise mentioned.
Calculation of Binding Constant (Ka) and analysis of thermodynamic parameters:

The binding constant (K
a ) and binding site number (n) of BSA/HSA with Ru:CNDEDAs are calculated using the following equation: [41]
(2)
log
F −
F 0
F
= log
K a
+ n
log
Q
where, F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher, and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. The values of K a and n are obtained from the intercept and slope of the plot of log(F 0 – F) / F against log[Q] respectively.

The thermodynamic parameters ∆H° and ∆S° for binding of BSA and HSA with Ru:CNDEDAs are determined from the van't Hoff equation:
(3)
ln
K a
= −
Δ H °
RT
+
Δ S °
R
where K a is the binding affinity constant, R and T are the universal gas constant and absolute temperature, respectively. The van't Hoff plot of lnK
a vs. 1/T gives a straight line. The standard enthalpy change (∆H°) and standard entropy change (∆S°) values are obtained from the slope (−∆H°/R) and intercept (∆S°/R) of the van't Hoff plot [42]. Using those parameters the standard free energy change (∆G°) is calculated from the following Eq. (4).
(4)
Δ G ° = Δ H ° − T Δ S °

The CD spectra of serum albumins are obtained by averaging four successive scans recorded at a scan speed of 50 nm min−1 over the range of 200–250 nm and subtracting the appropriate blanks (tris buffer) from these spectra. The results are expressed in terms of mean residual ellipticity (MRE) in degree (°) cm2 dmol−1 according to Eq. (5) [43]
(5)
MRE =
observed
CD
mdeg
C p
nl × 10
where C
p is the molar concentration of the protein, n is the number of amino acid residues (n = 583 for BSA and 585 for HSA [43]) and l is the path length (0.1 cm is the path length of the cuvette used). The α-Helical content of free and bound protein is evaluated from the MRE values at 222 nm using Eq. (6) [44].
(6)
α − Helix % =
−
MRE 222
+ 2340
30300
× 100
where, MRE 222 is the observed MRE value at 222 nm.

Serum albumins are found to interact with the carbon nano particles. In order to probe their interaction we have generated computational models. Average diameter of the carbon nano dot is ~100 nm whereas the average diameter of BSA/HSA is ~3 nm. Comparisons of the relative sizes are shown in the Fig. 1 . It is evident from their relative sizes that BSA/HSA interacts by adsorption on the surface of the nano materials. Therefore, we like to model a patch of the surface of the polymeric carbon nano dots. From the previously published literature [32,33] it is known that this type of carbon nano dot is made of large conjugated systems (Fig. S2) which form cross-link among themselves due to Michael addition reaction after addition of ethylene diamine. Based on this information we model a patch of the nano surface and placed BSA/HSA on it. The structural information of BSA (PDB ID: 3V03) [45] and HSA (PDB ID: 4L8U) [46] is obtained from Protein Data Bank. Protein structures are chosen based on the validation report provided by wwPDB at the PDB website [47]. Protein structures are prepared for MD according to previously published protocol [48]. All the hetero atoms and water and multiple subunits are removed from the PDB structures and the missing side chain residues for BSA are modeled at PDB_hydro web server [49]. UniProt numbering convention is followed. BSA (UniProt KB: P02769) has two tryptophan residues at position 158 and 237, respectively, whereas HSA (UniProt KB: P02768) has a single tryptophan at position 238 [50]. Structures of the building blocks for the construction of the nano surface are shown in S2. The building blocks and three outer layers of the nano surface are drawn in Avogadro [51] followed by energy minimization using steepest descent and conjugate gradient algorithms with universal force field (UFF) as implemented there.

The subsequent energy minimization of the complex is done in Schrodinger Maestro molecular modeling environment (academic release 2016-4). Molecular dynamics are performed using Desmond molecular dynamics program [52]. All the dynamic simulations are run under OPLS (optimized potentials for liquid simulations) force field [53]. The OPLS force field provides a broad coverage to the drug-like small molecules apart from the biomacromolecules [54], which makes it a suitable choice for the simulation of carbon dot-serum albumin complexes. Before the simulations in water media the complexes are subjected to a relaxation step within vaccuo for 100 ps with positional restrain on the bottom layer of the nano surface. The complexes are placed at the center of a periodic boundary box with a minimum distance of 10 A0 from each side. For the control experiments only BSA/HSA is used. These complexes/proteins are then solvated using SPC (single point charge) water model. Five stepped relaxation protocol is used before the production MD as described by the previously published literature [48,55,56]. For the production MD, a NPT ensemble is defined with a constant number of atoms (N), constant pressure (P) of 1 bar and a defined temperature of 300 K. Nose-Hoover thermostat and Martyna-Tobias-Klein barostat are used in this simulation with relaxation time of 1.0 and 2.0 ps, respectively. The production MDs are run for 48 ns each for the different complexes and the free proteins. Simulation trajectory analysis to probe the deviations from initial conformation (RMSD), radius of gyration (Rg), hydrogen bonding with the nano surface, distances from the tryptophans and orientation of the tryptophan rings with respect to the nano surface etc. is performed in Schrodinger Maestro and Visual Molecular Dynamics [52] software of University of Illinois.

Fig. 2 (a) and (b) depicts the absorption spectra of BSA and HSA proteins with gradually increasing concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs from 0 μM to 2.5 μM in PBS buffer. Insets of those figures represent the relative changes in the absorbance value at 360 nm where a new band is generated with increasing concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs. As it is already known that Ru:CNDEDAs have absorption band around 350 nm [32], hence it becomes important to monitor solely the change in absorbance of proteins or related newly generated species without any unnecessary effect contributed from Ru:CNDEDAs. This can be easily achieved by simultaneous addition of same amount of Ru:CNDEDAs solution both at sample and reference cuvettes for each experimental set, therefore whatever the change is found could be solely attributed by the protein and the ground-state complex, if any, formed through the interaction of Ru:CNDEDAs with proteins. Therefore, it can be inferred that the new band which is generated at 360 nm is probably due to the formation of ground state complex between proteins and Ru:CNDEDAs. The emergence of both the peaks at 360 nm and 250 nm could be assigned to the formation of ground state complex between Ru:CNDEDAs and protein. Each of these complex forming partners has multipart structure and can give rise to the complex absorbing at varied wavelengths. Therefore, we find an overall increase in absorption spectrum while adding Ru:CNDEDAs to protein devoid of any prominent isosbestic point. From the relative change of the absorbance value at 360 nm for the respective protein it is clear that the ground state complex formation occurs in a regular manner for BSA protein with Ru:CNDEDAs as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(a), however for HSA protein, the scenario is quite different. After certain concentration of added Ru:CNDEDAs as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(b), the trend for variation in absorbance changes in case of HSA and is not as regular as BSA. Therefore, it might be concluded that the stability of the ground state complex of Ru:CNDEDAs with BSA is much more than with HSA as reflected from the enhancement of relative absorbance value for the respective proteins. We have also performed a control experiment where Ru:CNDEDAs are added in same ratio as that is used for previous experiments. Control experiment shows the peak of the Ru:CNDEDAs is at 352 nm and enhancement of absorbance is more than that of Ru:CNDEDAs in presence of BSA/HSA shown in Fig. S1.

The concentrations of Ru:CNDEDAs, which are used for UV–Vis, steady- state fluorescence, time-resolved fluorescence, and circular dichroism experiments, are calculated in terms of absorbance of Ru:CNDEDAs at a particular wavelength (350 nm) as shown in Table-S1.

CD is a very sensitive and powerful tool to study the influence of ligand on the secondary structures of proteins. Fig. 3(a) and (b) represents the changes in the CD spectra of BSA and HSA respectively in absence and presence of Ru:CNDEDAs which account for the alteration in the secondary structure of the proteins on binding. Native BSA and HSA exhibit two minima at 208 nm (π-π*) and 222 nm (n-π*) which are the characteristics of a typical α-helical structure [57]. It is observed that upon addition of Ru:CNDEDAs, the intensity of the bands at both 208 and 222 nm decreases which implies a decrease in the α-helical content of the protein.

It is customary to express the helical content of a protein in terms of mean residual ellipticity (MRE) at 222 nm. The α-helical content of model proteins i.e. BSA and HSA, in absence and presence of Ru:CNDEDAs has been calculated and summarized in Tables 1a and 1b
.

It is evident that the α-helical content of BSA decreases from 47% to 41.02% and that of HSA decreases from 61.73% to 57.95% indicating destabilization of protein structures of both the serum albumins [41] in presence of Ru:CNDEDAs. In the insets of Fig. 3a and b, the spectra in the region 200–230 nm for individual protein are magnified and presented.

Fig. 4a depicts the effect of increasing concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs on the fluorescence spectrum of BSA protein. With the addition of Ru:CNDEDAs to BSA, the fluorescence intensity of BSA at 350 nm decreases with the concomitant enhancement of Ru:CNDEDAs emission around 450 nm through an isoemissive point at 396 nm with a slight blue shift of emission maximum of the protein from 340 nm to 335 nm. This blue shift indicates that the Trp residues of the protein experience a hydrophobic environment in the presence of Ru:CNDEDAs. Similar observation is obtained in Fig. 4b which shows that the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA is quenched without any shift of fluorescence maximum along with increase in fluorescence intensity of Ru:CNDEDAs emission around 450 nm with an associated isoemissive point around 400 nm. However, it is evident from Fig. 4a and b that quenching of tryptophan fluorescence of BSA is more effective than that of HSA on interaction with Ru:CNDEDAs. Further, enhancement in fluorescence of Ru:CNDEDAs is more prominent in case of interaction with BSA compared to that with HSA.

The quenching of fluorescence of the protein accompanied by enhancement in fluorescence of Ru:CNDEDAs along with emergence of an isoemissive point gives an indication of occurrence of energy transfer from the Trp residue of the protein to Ru:CNDEDAs. The primary condition of radiative Fӧrster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is that the emission spectrum of donor (BSA/HSA) must overlap with absorption spectrum of the acceptor (Ru:CNDEDAs), which is found to be fulfilled. However, the most convincing proof of FRET has been shown by decrease in fluorescence lifetime of the proteins in presence of Ru:CNDEDAs as shown in Tables 2a and 2b
. Barik et al. and Patel et al. have previously verified the occurrence of FRET by observing the reduction in lifetime of the donor on addition of the acceptor moiety [58,59].

Another alternative proof of confirmation of FRET is monitoring the enhancement in lifetime of acceptor following donor excitation. In the present case, increase in lifetime at 450 nm, which corresponds to the emission of RuCNDEDAs, is observed when a solution containing the RuCNDEDAs and protein are excited at 295 nm as shown from Tables 3a and 3b
. It is evident from Tables 3a and 3b that lifetime of the acceptor increases markedly in presence of BSA compared to HSA, which probably indicates that energy transfer is much more efficient in case of Ru:CNDEDA-BSA system compared to Ru:CNDEDA-HSA. Using the steady-state fluorescence data, the efficiency of energy transfer has been calculated to be 34.26% and 16.23% for Ru:CNDEDA-BSA and Ru:CNDEDA-HSA systems respectively.
Conventionally, the analysis of fluorescence quenching data is done using SV equation [60].

The SV analysis for fluorescence quenching of HSA with varied concentration of Ru:CNDEDAs shows a linear plot whereas that for BSA, a linear plot with minor negative deviation is obtained. The linearity in SV plot is obvious as there is a clear evidence of ground state complex formation (Fig. 2a and b) leading to static quenching of the fluorescence of serum albumins (Figs. 5a and 5b). However, in case of BSA non linearity arises in SV plot to some extent which may be due to effective FRET from Trp moiety of BSA to Ru:CNDEDAs. The signature of nonlinearity is not prominent in case of HSA which has lower efficiency of FRET compared to BSA in presence of Ru:CNDEDAs as discussed in previous section. The efficiency of energy transfer over the ground state complex formation can be understood much more precisely from time-resolved studies which mainly focus the excited state phenomena. The changes in fluorescence lifetime of BSA and HSA in absence and in presence of Ru:CNDEDAs are shown in Tables 2a and 2b. Considering the low concentration range of Ru:CNDEDAs where the SV plot for steady-state fluorescence is linear [60], the values of SV constants (K
SV ) and quenching rate constants (k
q ) are determined for fluorescence quenching of both BSA and HSA. Furthermore, the values of K
SV and k
q are also calculated using the data obtained from time-resolved fluorescence study (Table 4 ), where the order of k
q values, which is even higher than the values obtained from steady-state fluorescence quenching, indicates the occurrence of FRET between Ru:CNDEDAs and serum albumins in excited state. As evident from Table 4, the values of k
q are higher than the limiting diffusion collision quenching constant, suggesting the mechanism of fluorescence quenching in both the proteins in the presence of Ru:CNDEDAs is static [61].

The estimations of K
a and n are done using Eq. (2) based on analysis of fluorescence quenching data. Furthermore, the thermodynamic parameters such as ∆G°, ∆H° and ∆S° associated with the interaction of Ru:CNDEDAs with serum albumins are evaluated using Eqs. (3) and (4). Tables 5a and 5b
summarize the values of various binding and thermodynamic parameters at various temperatures using fluorescence quenching data.

Table 5a suggests the exothermicity of Ru:CNDEDA-BSA interaction whereas Table 5b suggests that Ru:CNDEDA-HSA interaction is endothermic in nature. However, both the tables suggest the overall negative Gibb's free energy change indicating the spontaneity of interaction for both the systems. It is observed that the value of K a decreases with increase in temperature for Ru:CNDEDA-BSA interaction implying the formation of a complex between BSA and Ru:CNDEDAs which loses its stability at higher temperature and also confirms the static nature of fluorescence quenching. On the contrary, the value of K
a increases with increase in temperature for Ru:CNDEDA-HSA which is unusual for static quenching. However, quenching efficiency should vary proportionately with temperature according to Arrhenius theory. Thus, in the case of Ru:CNDEDA-HSA interaction, the increase in K a with increasing temperature implies that the effect of increase of K a owing to increase in temperature is greater than the decrease of K a due to instability of the complex. Such an unusual observation of adverse temperature dependence of K a in static quenching process has been reported by Rudra et.al and Ghosh et al. while studying the interaction of bovine β‑Lactoglobulin with strychnine and chloramphenicol respectively [62,63]. The value of K
a is found to be higher for Ru:CNDEDA-BSA system than Ru:CNDEDA-HSA system. Furthermore, negative values of ∆H° and ∆S° suggest the predominance of van der Waals interaction and hydrogen bonding in the binding interaction of Ru:CNDEDA-BSA system, while positive values of ∆H° and ∆S° imply the involvement of hydrophobic forces of interaction in Ru:CNDEDA-HSA system [64].

Experimental studies suggest the formation of ground state complex of both the serum albumins with the amine coated surface of the carbon nano dot. In order to probe the stability of such a complex, a computation model has been built up. Previous report suggests that the carbon nano dot is composed of various chromophoric units formed by citric acid pyrolyzation [32]. These chromophoric units are rich in keto and carboxylic acid groups. Therefore, addition of diamine leads to the cross-linking of the fluorophores and the formation of amine coated carbon nano particles. The surface of such a nano particle is rich in protruding side chains with terminal amines. Based on these information an amine rich surface patch has been modeled and the BSA and HSA are docked on to it (Fig. 1) followed by molecular dynamics simulation in SPC water for 48 ns. Trajectories of these simulations are documented in the Videos S1 and S2 for BSA and HSA, respectively. The trajectory is further analyzed for overall structural deviations, radius of gyration, amino acid fluctuations, secondary structural elements and the distances of tryptophans from the nano surface.

Experimental studies suggest the formation of ground state complex of both the serum albumins with the amine coated surface of the carbon nano dot. In order to probe the stability of such a complex, a computation model has been built up. Previous report suggests that the carbon nano dot is composed of various chromophoric units formed by citric acid pyrolyzation [32]. These chromophoric units are rich in keto and carboxylic acid groups. Therefore, addition of diamine leads to the cross-linking of the fluorophores and the formation of amine coated carbon nano particles. The surface of such a nano particle is rich in protruding side chains with terminal amines. Based on these information an amine rich surface patch has been modeled and the BSA and HSA are docked on to it (Fig. 1) followed by molecular dynamics simulation in SPC water for 48 ns. Trajectories of these simulations are documented in the Videos S1 and S2 for BSA and HSA, respectively. The trajectory is further analyzed for overall structural deviations, radius of gyration, amino acid fluctuations, secondary structural elements and the distances of tryptophans from the nano surface.

Fig. 6 shows the overall structural changes that takes place in BSA and HSA on the nano surface with respect to their initial conformations over the course of simulation. On the surface of the carbon nano dot, the structural changes in HSA are found to be more than that of BSA. However, both BSA and HSA rapidly reach a steady equilibrium conformation on the nano surface.

Fig. 7 shows the changes in the radius of gyration of the two proteins. Radius of gyration (r
g ) is the average distance of all the atoms in a molecule from the centroid. Thus, the r
g is a measure of the protein flexibility. When a protein opens up the r
g increases and when a protein becomes compact the r
g decreases. From the simulation of BSA and HSA on the nano surface, it is found that the r
g of BSA increases very slightly (0.5 Å), whereas, the r
g of HSA remains almost the same. In fact, appreciable change in the r
g value is not found for both the serum albumins (as depicted in Fig. 7). The changes in the RMSD and r
g are actually a cumulative reflection of the fluctuations of the amino acid residues in the proteins.

Residuewise backbone and side chain fluctuations in BSA and HSA are computed from the MD trajectory (shown in Fig. 8a and b). When bound to the nano surface some regions of BSA and HSA show more fluctuation than their free forms. However, in BSA bound to nano surface, the fluctuations of both the tryptophan residues are found to be similar to that of the BSA in solution (Fig. 8a). In contrast to BSA, the helix to which tryptophan residue is attached in HSA shows slightly more fluctuations when bound to nano surface than in water (Fig. 8b).

The CD experiments show that the helical content decreases for both the proteins when they bind to the nano surface. Therefore, we have monitored the changes in the secondary structural components (helix and random coil) over the simulation time as shown in Fig. 9 . It is found that for both the proteins helical contents decrease and the random coil contents increase over time upon binding to the nano surface. Around 4% of helix becomes uncoiled to form random structure i.e. around 23 amino acids are transformed from the ordered helical state to disordered conformation upon adsorption to the amine coated carbon nano surface. Fig. 9 also highlights the residue that goes through this helix to coil transitions transiently or permanently. Structural propensities of the tryptophan residues in both the proteins are, however, for the helix only.

A residue of structural propensity plot is computed from the MD trajectory and shown in the Fig. 10 . The positions of the tryptophan residues are marked in these plots, which clearly show that those remain in the helical conformation for 100% of the time after adsorption to the nano surface. However, structural changes in the nearby regions are numerous (Fig. 10) which may leads to the altered microenvironment of tryptophan leading to the changes in its fluorescence behavior.

The distances of the tryptophan residues from the nano surface have also been computed from the MD trajectory (Fig. 11 ). The tryptophans are found to be 20–30 Å away from the nano surface and remain within the range of optimum distance required for FRET, thus substantiating the energy transfer process. However, the higher FRET efficiency for BSA might be due to the local environment of tryptophan in BSA than in HSA. Mitra et al. reported that the cavity near tryptophan in HSA is almost twice as big as that of in BSA [50]. In absence of small molecule ligand, these cavities are filled up with water, hence can directly alter the environment of the buried tryptophan in both the BSA and HSA. In HSA, however, the change could be more prominent due to the larger cavity near the tryptophan residue. Further, we have observed more backbone fluctuations in this region of HSA in bound state than in free-state (Fig. 10b). The resultant of these cumulative effects could decrease FRET efficiency of HSA compared to BSA.

The present study primarily focuses on differential behavior of Ru (III) doped carbon nano dots with BSA and HSA. UV–Vis absorption spectroscopic study shows that Ru:CNDEDAs form ground state complex with both the model proteins, however, the stability of Ru:CNDEDA-BSA complex is higher than that of Ru:CNDEDA-HSA complex. An indication of energy transfer between Ru:CNDEDAs and the model proteins is obtained from steady state fluorescence study. A comparative study of the spectra for the two systems clearly indicates that the energy transfer is more efficient in Ru:CNDEDA-BSA system rather than in Ru:CNDEDA-HSA system, which has been substantiated by the calculated values of ‘efficiency of energy transfer’. The occurrence of energy transfer has been authenticated by decrease in lifetime of the donors (serum albumin) in nanosecond regime. Earlier reports suggest that BSA has a more rigid structure compared to HSA in the solution phase. Thus, it may be proposed that the rigid structure of BSA imparts stability to Ru:CNDEDA-BSA complex while the flexible structure of HSA is a hindrance in formation of stable Ru:CNDEDA-HSA complex and this is subsequently reflected in the energy transfer efficiency for both the systems. Simulation study also confirmed that Trp residue of BSA/HSA are in the FRET distance from Ru:CNDEDAs surface thus substantiating the energy transfer process. However, more backbone fluctuation of HSA than BSA on the surface decreases the energy transfer efficiency for Ru:CNDEDA-HSA system compared to Ru:CNDEDA-BSA system. The values of association constant for Ru:CNDEDA-BSA system is found to be higher than that of Ru:CNDEDA-HSA system. Although, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching in both the systems is static, the values of K a for Ru:CNDEDA-BSA system decreases with increase in temperature, which is normal for static quenching, but the values of K
a for Ru:CNDEDA-HSA system increases with increase in temperature, which is unusual for static quenching. This adverse temperature dependence of K
a has been rationalized in the light of Arrhenius theory. The results of molecular modeling and dynamic simulation studies support our experimental findings, which eventually prove the preferential photochemical interaction of Ru (III) doped carbon nano dots with BSA over HSA.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Video S1
Trajectories of molecular dynamic simulations for BSA.
Video S1
Video S2
Trajectories of molecular dynamic simulations for HSA.
Video S2
Supplementary material
Image 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2019.06.126.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Biomolecular Assembly, Recognition and Dynamics (BARD) project, SINP of Department of Atomic Energy (DAE), Government of India is greatly acknowledged.

Spectroscopic exploration of drug–protein interaction: a study highlighting the dependence of the magnetic field effect on inter-radical separationdistance formed during photoinduced electron transfer. RSC Adv. 2015, 5, 81533-81545.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interaction of curcumin with human serum albumin: thermodynamic properties, fluorescence energy transfer and denaturation effects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Barik, A.; Mishra, B.; Kunwar, A.; Priyadarsini, K. I. Interaction of curcumin with human serum albumin: Thermodynamic properties, fluorescence energy transfer and denaturation effects. Chem. Phys. Lett. 2007, 436, 239–243.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Steady state and time-resolved fluorescence investigation of the specific binding of two chlorin derivatives with human serum albumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Patel, S.; Datta, A. Steady State and Time-resolved Fluorescence Investigation of the Specific Binding of Two Chlorin Derivatives with Human Serum Albumin. J. Phys. Chem. B 2007, 111, 10557–10562.
J.R. Lakowicz, Principles of Fluorescence Spectroscopy, third ed., pp 278-279, Springer, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Understanding the inhibitory mechanism of tea polyphenols against tyrosinase using fluorescence spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry, oximetry, and molecular simulations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tang, H.; Cui, F.; Li, H.; Huang, Q.; Li, Y. Understanding the inhibitory mechanism of tea polyphenols against tyrosinase using fluorescence spectroscopy, cyclic voltametry, oximetry,, and molecular simulations. RSC Adv. 2018, 8, 8310-8318.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization of the binding of strychnine with bovine β‑lactoglobulin and human lysozyme using spectroscopic, kinetic and molecular docking analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Rudra, S., Jana, A.; Sepay, N.; Patel, B.K.; Mahapatra, A. Charecterization of the binding of strychnine with bovine β-Lactoglobulin and human lysozyme using spectroscopic, kinetic and molecular docking analysis. New J. Chem. 2018, 42, 8615-8628.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inverse temperature dependence in static quenching versus calorimetric exploration: binding interaction of chloramphenicol to β‑lactoglobulin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ghosh, N.; Mondal, R.; Mukherjee, S. Inverse Temperature Dependence in Static Quenching versus Calorimetric Exploration: Binding Interaction of Chloramphenicol to β-Lactoglobulin. Langmuir 2015, 31, 8074-8080.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermodynamics of protein association reactions: forces contributing to stability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ross, P. D.; Subhramanian, S. Thermodynamics of protein association reactions: forces contributing to stability. Biochemistry 1981, 20, 3096–3102.
